figures.caption: Fig. 6: Ablation summary across four factors: perturbation repeat R, probe layer, perturbation type, and retriever backbone.
Fig. 2: Overall retrieval-stage results, comparing Poison Hit Rate and Poison Recall Rate under sθ(q, p) and s̃R,θ,ϑ(q, p).
Fig. 5: Ablation of the reranking objective. We compare the full score in Eq. (13) against ablated variants obtained by removing wθ(q, p), P R,θ,ϑ

Fig. 1: Comparison between the undefended ranking pipeline and our proposed probe-gradient reranking. ProGRank extracts two sensitivity-based penalties, a consistency penalty and a saturated risk penalty, and fuses them with a score gate to obtain the defended reranking score, thereby suppressing poisoned passages and reducing poisoned Top-K exposure.

Table 2: Downstream generation results at Top-5. Each cell reports substringbased ASR (top), LLM-judged ASR (middle), and substring-based ACC (bottom). For each row, the lowest substring-based ASR and LLM-judged ASR, as well as the highest ACC, are highlighted in bold.

Fig. 4: Rank shift induced by ProGRank. Upward and downward rank shifts are reported separately for poisoned and clean passages as Poison Up, Poison Down, Clean Up, and Clean Down. Top row shows maximum shift magnitude and the bottom row shows mean shift magnitude. We use P ↓ and C ↓ as shorthand for Poison Down and Clean Down, respectively.
